coredata.dc:description: Nanotechnology has experienced substantial expansion due to its wide-ranging applications, and in view of this, different synthetic routes were developed and many of them employ toxic reagents, have high energy consumption and generate toxic waste. Nowadays, with growing concerns regarding environmental and health safety, green chemistry is gaining prominence and as a result, certain synthetic routes have been replaced to align with the principles of green chemistry. This review outlines current methods for synthesizing silver, iron, and carbon nanoparticles, alongside discussions on green synthesis routes and their mechanisms. Additionally, it evaluates future perspectives on green synthesis, addressing the challenges that need to be overcome.

row.entry: CNTs have exceptional mechanical properties, such as an exceptionally high modulus of elasticity, high strength, and superior thermal and electrical properties. They are thermally stable up to 2800 °C in vacuum, have high electrical conductivity and a thermal conductivity twice that of diamond
composite fillers in polymers to improve the mechanical, thermal and electrical properties of the resulting composites
Size of 10 nm, it exhibits excellent luminescence properties, including low toxicity, photostability, biocompatibility, multiphoton excitation, up-converted photoluminescence, and chemiluminescence.
biomedical and energy applications, particularly in supramolecular systems

Hollow cage nanomaterials are characterized by electrical conductivity, electron affinity, structure, strength, and versatility. They have pentagonal and hexagonal carbon units linked by sp2 hybridized carbon atoms.
IT devices, diagnostics, pharmaceuticals, environmental and energy industries

It can be wrapped into 0-dimensional fullerenes, rolled into 1-dimensional carbon nanotubes, and stacked into 3-dimensional graphite. Excellent thermal conductivity, high electron mobility at room temperature, very high light transmittance, excellent gas impermeability
From medical to paper to electronics to composite materials

head.ce:title: Advancing nanomaterial synthesis: Harnessing green chemistry for sustainable innovation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanotechnology has experienced substantial expansion due to its wide-ranging applications, and in view of this, different synthetic routes were developed and many of them employ toxic reagents, have high energy consumption and generate toxic waste. Nowadays, with growing concerns regarding environmental and health safety, green chemistry is gaining prominence and as a result, certain synthetic routes have been replaced to align with the principles of green chemistry. This review outlines current methods for synthesizing silver, iron, and carbon nanoparticles, alongside discussions on green synthesis routes and their mechanisms. Additionally, it evaluates future perspectives on green synthesis, addressing the challenges that need to be overcome.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology involves the manipulation of matter at the nanoscale for various applications in chemistry, materials science, engineering, and medicine. This area has seen significant growth owing to its diverse applications, with the global nanotechnology market projected to reach at least 3 trillion by 2020. Among the dynamic aspects of nanotechnology is the synthesis of nanoparticles (NPs), as these particles exhibit unique or improved properties attributable to their size, shape, and structure at the nanoscale [1]. Because of their easy synthesization and the possibility to control their composition, size, and functionalization, NPs are used in different applications such as adsorbent of environmental contaminants, drug delivery, and many others [2].

Given the significance of nanoparticle synthesis in advancing nanotechnology, there's a growing emphasis on employing methodologies that prioritize both human health and environmental safety. Consequently, approaches aligned with green chemistry principles are increasingly favored in this regard. The green chemistry principles were developed by Paul Anastas and John Warner and they focus on the development of more environment-friendly chemical processes or products. These principles aim at the prevention of pollution before it can occur by minimizing the use of hazardous chemicals which are illustrated in Fig. 1 , which was developed in 1998 by Anastas and Warner [3].

Considering these principles, some changes in conventional methods for NPs synthesis can be made such as: (i) to stabilize and reduce ions to form metal nanoparticles, non-toxic reducing reagents such as citrate, β-d-glucose, carboxymethyl cellulose sodium, chitosan [4], plant [5], and microorganism extracts, such as bacteria [6] and fungi [7–11], can be used; (ii) organic solvents, which can be toxic and pollute the environment, can be changed to aqueous solutions; (iii) high energy inputs (heat sources such as water/oil bath and heating mantle) can be changed by synthesis at room temperature, which needs low energy inputs; (iv) some substances can be extracted from microorganisms, which can be a renewable source of reactants [12]. The advancement of these methodologies based on the green chemistry methods is evident in the scientific literature. Fig. 2 A illustrates the distribution and connections of clusters using VOSviewer with data from the Scopus database. The clusters define areas potentially associated with the use of NPs for catalysis (blue), the application and development of methodologies for remediation and pollutant control (green), and the antimicrobial activity of pathogens (red). These clusters highlight the growing demand (Fig. 2B) and the increasing interdisciplinary nature research through the distribution of publication area (Fig. 2C).

Although various green methodologies for synthesizing nanoparticles have been developed and reported [13], there is a lack of comprehensive discussion on the mechanisms of these green synthesis methods compared to classical methodologies. This review aims to fill this gap by bringing significant advancement in the field by addressing the shift towards green chemistry in nanotechnology besides providing an in-depth analysis and discussion of the mechanisms involved in green synthesis. Additionally, it highlights the future perspectives on this important subject.

In ancient civilizations, bulk silver was commonly applied to surfaces for its striking color variations. This effect arose from the unintentional formation of nanoparticles of varying sizes and shapes when metal chloride was mixed with molten glass. This early phenomenon represents one of the earliest known applications of nanotechnology involving silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [5]. The first report of AgNPs was made over 130 years ago, in 1889 by M. C. Lea, who synthesized citrate-stabilized silver colloids with an average diameter of 7–9 nm, and nowadays it is estimated that the production of AgNPs is about 320 tons/year [14]. The high production of AgNPs illustrates the wide application of these NPs, since they are the most interesting material for industrial applications acting as antibacterial agents and being important for electronic, medicinal [15], food industries [16], and also for biosensor applications [17]. Considering this wide range of applications, there are different methodologies to synthesize these NPs which are discussed in the following topics.

The synthesis of AgNPs is critical because their size, structure, and chemical and physical properties heavily rely on the chosen synthetic method. Various approaches have been explored, including chemical, physical, and biological methodologies. Chemical methods, particularly chemical reduction, electrochemical techniques, and pyrolysis methods, are commonly used for AgNPs synthesis. Among these, chemical reduction stands out as the most employed method, involving the reduction of Ag+ species to Ag° using reducing agents such as citrate [18], NaBH4 [19], and ascorbate [20]. Although these methodologies are widely employed, most of them use toxic reagents and also generate toxic residues. The physical methods do not use toxic compounds; however, they require high energy consumption, which is a drawback of this approach [3]. Fig. 3 lists some chemical and physical methods [21–28].

In addition to these various methodologies for synthesizing AgNPs, it is crucial to consider their stability. In this regard, different stabilizing agents can be employed, and both reducing and capping agents play pivotal roles in stabilizing AgNPs and controlling their size [29]. Capping agents stabilize the NPs by different mechanisms such as electrostatic, steric, stabilization by hydration forces, van der Waals forces, and also depletion stabilization. Besides the stabilization main role, some capping agents (such as citrate) also can reduce the Ag ions into metal NPs, and therefore, the choice of stabilizing agent for AgNPs synthesis is crucial since it also affects their size, shape, and interaction with the solvent [30].

The morphology, and consequently, the size of NPs depends on the thermodynamic equilibrium, which is affected by the pH: in high pH, the primary particles have a large surface charge which prevents aggregation (in combination with the stabilizing agents such as citrate ions) providing particles with small size Decreasing the pH, the system approaches the isoelectric point becoming less stable and promoting aggregation and, consequently, large particles [31,32].

Considering that chemical methods can involve toxic compounds and generate toxic residues and physical methods require high energy consumption, an alternative is to employ greener methodologies such as biological synthesis which uses bacteria, fungi, plants, and other organisms. The green methods besides involving compounds instead of toxic reactants and in most cases carried out at room temperature, also can control the size and shape of the AgNPs due to changes in incubation time, concentration of bio extracts, pH, and concentration of AgNO3.

The use of plants and plant extracts for AgNPs synthesis has been widely used due to the efficiency in reduction and stabilization of NPs synthesized [33]. Furthermore, the synthesis by plants has advantages compared to microbial synthesis due to its no need for culture maintenance of cells, quickness, and contamination-free [34].

The mechanism of synthesis of AgNPs by plants involves the interaction of silver nitrate and the plant extracts: the Ag+ ions are reduced which leads to silver nuclei formation and, consequently, the AgNPs formation. The reduction and also stabilization of NPs are due to the presence of hydroxyl groups in different plant biomolecules such as flavonoids, saponins, tannins, polyphenols, and carbohydrates [35].

The control of shape, size, and size distribution is affected by metal ion concentration, subtract concentration, pH, and production time. According to Rahuman et al. low concentrations of AgNO3 in the synthesis can lead to larger AgNPs as well as the increase of plant extract produced AgNPs in small size due to the quickness of the reaction which forms more silver nuclei and leads to AgNPs in small size [36]. The pH affects the size of NPs because ate low pH, the reduction of Ag+ to Ag0 occurs by the interaction with amino and sulfhydryl groups which is followed by ionic bonding with the biomolecules resulting in agglomeration and larger sizes of AgNPs while at higher pH occurs a quick bioreduction resulting in lower sizes of AgNPs [28]. The incubation period also impacts the AgNPs characteristics: the increase in incubation time also increases the AgNPs size due to the formation of agglomerates [37].

Recently, Karan et al. synthesized AgNPs with average size of 18 nm from Sambucus ebulus leaves extract which Ag were reduced, capped, and stabilized by the secondary metabolites found in the water extract of the plant [38]. Raphanus sativus L. leaf aqua extract was employed to obtain AgNPs with average diameter of 16 nm and spherical shape. Furthermore, the authors reported cytotoxic and anti-pathogenic potential of these synthesized AgNPs [39]. Furthermore, microwave irradiation was employed for AgNPs synthesis by plants in a fast green synthesis. Magdy et al. used the extract of Piper longum fruit as a reducing agent and the AgNPs synthesis, measuring 27.3 nm, was accomplished in about 30 s [40]. Kadam et al. also employed microwave and sunlight irradiation for synthesize AgNPs from F. schweinfurthi Hadidi in vivo and in vitro biomass [41]. Tomato skins were employed for AgNPs green synthesis, with a diameter ranging from 21 to 93 nm, according to Carbone at al., and they observed catalytic activity in the degradation of methylene blue [42]. Giri et al. synthesized AgNPs, with average size of 24 nm, from Eugenia roxburghii DC. Extract. These NPs were very effective against biofilm production by bacteria, although tests of toxicity must be performed before their use in animals and humans [43]. As reported by Vanlalveni at al. an interesting effect was observed from AgNPs synthesized by plants and their application for antimicrobial activity. The shape of NPs influences the antimicrobial activity following the order triangular > pentagonal, hexagonal, cubic, nano-rod > spherical, since triangular nanoparticles have sharp edge and predominant stable (1 1 1) facet, whereas spherical shape have no sharp edge, presenting the lowest antimicrobial effect [44].

Green AgNPs can also be synthesized by microorganisms such as bacteria and fungi. When bacteria get exposed to some conditions that can affect their metabolism, they develop biochemical mechanisms to survive in this extreme environment, for example in the presence of Ag salts. Some bacteria can reduce Ag+ to Ag0 by different biomolecules such as proteins, enzymes, sugars, and amino acids leading to the accumulation of Ag0 in the form of AgNPs inside the cell, and in some cases, NADH-dependent nitrate reductase enzymes play an important role in this process. Furthermore, the presence of carboxylate groups in the cell surface possesses a negative charge which attracts the Ag+ to the cell surface [45]. Saravanan et al. synthesized AgNPs from Bacillus brevis and obtained NPs with spherical shapes and sizes ranging from 41 to 68 nm. Furthermore, they reported that AgNPs production was initiated within a few minutes and was completed after 1 h at room temperature [46]. The soil bacteria Cupriavidus sp. was employed in the synthesis of spherical and small (5 - 50 nm) AgNPs, moreover, the presence of amides in the AgNPs suggests that these biomolecules act in the reduction of Ag+ to Ag0 [47].

AgNPs can be synthesized by fungi by intracellular and extracellular methods. In the intracellular method, AgNPs are synthesized under the cell surface at mycelia and are normally larger than those synthesized extracellularly due to the nucleation inside the organism. The extracellular approach employs a fungal cell-free filtrate for nanoparticle synthesis. This method has some advantages, when compared with the intracellular methodology, such as providing a wide range and easy control of particle size, and easy recovery of synthesized AgNPs, because in the intracellular method, the NPs tend to attach to the biomass cell [48]. This extracellular method was employed for AgNPs synthesis by Talaromyces purpureogenus and 16 compounds were identified in its extract, such as phenols and terpenoids, responsible for reducing Ag+ into Ag0 and forming AgNPs with an average size of 30–60 nm [49]. Recently, Kumar et al. reported that Colletotrichum gloeosporioides extract was used to synthesize multi-twinned silver nanoparticles with an average size of 13 nm by the extracellular method after 12 h of incubation at room temperature [50]. Rilda et al. recently synthesized Ag-doped ZnO nanorods using extracellular enzymes of Bacillus sp. with size ranging from 126 – 199.146 nm. The author also observed the antifungal activity of NPs and reported that the smaller the size of the nanoparticles, the larger the inhibition zone of Candida albicans [7].

In the field of nanomaterials synthesis, iron-based nanoparticles (FeNPs) have garnered significant interest due to their wide range of potential applications, including biomedical [51], environmental remediation [52], catalytic activities [53]. Consequently, the growing demand for these nanoparticles has driven the development of various methods for their synthesis and stabilization, utilizing physical, chemical, and biological approaches.

FeNPs can be synthesized using either top-down processes, which involve breaking down macro- and micro-structured minerals, or bottom-up processes, which involve manipulating atoms to build structures [54]. In the bottom-up method, FeNPs are constructed through controlled chemical reactions, allowing for precise control over the nanomaterial's morphological and structural properties. This method can also be adapted to incorporate components that stabilize the nanoparticles and prevent further growth. Conversely, the top-down method requires specialized equipment to manage the laser ablation process. Pulverization or milling techniques used in this approach pose contamination risks. While these methods can regulate the size of the nanomaterials, they often fail to ensure consistent control over their structural properties, resulting in varied characteristics among individual particles.

To achieve the desired magnetic properties, toxic reducing agents like sodium borohydride are often used. However, these agents limit the direct use of FeNPs in humans and generate chemical waste that requires treatment before disposal. As a result, "green" production methods (Fig. 4 ) have emerged as an ideal solution for producing FeNPs. Additionally, techniques such as co-precipitation enhance the efficiency of FeNPs synthesis while reducing environmental impact.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthetic routes and bioactive agents
ce:section.ce:para: Green synthetic routes aim to reduce toxic waste by modifying traditional syntheses through solvent alteration [55]. In the field of studying and producing FeNPs, it is common practice to stabilize the oxidation state of the precursor metal or apply components to ensure steric stability of the iron nanoparticles in dispersion. Conventional synthesis often leads to residues with very low pH because the iron has to be thermally stabilized and because of the large amount of organic matter that controls the nucleation dynamics of the NM. To produce FeNPs in an environmentally friendly way, the synthesizing reagents should be modified to reduce the toxicological potential of the wastes. One promising strategy is the use of bioagents (enzymes or extracts from bacteria, fungi and plants).

Bioagent methods have distinct advantages over conventional protocols. The criteria for evaluating the processes include environmental safety, reduced reagent demand, minimal or no generation of toxic agents, economic viability, and ease of process control and maintenance [56–58]. Table 1 provides examples of organisms or extracts used in the synthesis of FeNPs.

The mechanisms underlying the production of bioactive compounds remain unclear due to the complexity of composition, metabolism and components. However, common pathways, such as bioreduction by polyphenols, are typically involved in the process of electron valence bioreduction, growth and stabilization [66–68]. Polyphenols are macromolecules that contain aromatic rings with hydroxyl groups as substituents. These hydroxyl groups act as hard ligands that can reduce hard (Fe3+) and intermediate/soft metals (Fe2+), while the carbonyl groups act as soft ligands that can coordinate with zero-valent iron. Consequently, the polyphenolic structure has two distinct properties: reducing the oxidation state and providing electrosteric stabilization [69]. These properties control the process of nucleation and growth of nanoparticles, making polyphenols essential agents in green chemistry methodologies.

Asif Asghar [70] found a 1:1 proportionality between the oxidation state of metals (Iron, Copper, and Silver) and the number of moles of phenolic (Camellia sinensis extract) compounds associated with the reduction. Zero-valent iron nanoparticles can be obtained in the resulting acidic medium along with reduced aromatic compounds. According to Becerra [71], gallic acid derived from alfalfa can complex trivalent iron and carry out the reduction, generating Wuesite (FeO, Fe(III) to Fe(II)) and Magnetite (Fe3O4, incomplete reduction in which the presence of both oxidations states leads to the formation of the nanomineral with mixed oxidation number). In contrast, Wang [72] observed that position 4- and 8- of flavonoids derived from Salvia officinalis are active. These positions can be condensed to create a functional structure that can complex Fe(III). Fig. 5 illustrates a general mechanism. However, this mechanism is not completely clear and scientifically accepted. According to spectroscopic evidence, polyphenols cannot convert ionic iron to zero-valent iron. Instead, they produce iron-polyphenol complexes with amorphous structures [73–75].

Green chemistry is a promising approach for recycling and reducing industrial waste, particularly when the necessary precursors for synthesizing nanomaterials can be obtained from waste produced during industrial processes [77]

Samadda et al. [78] highlights the importance of pre-treating waste for synthesis applications using physical, chemical, or mixed methods to make the analyte available for the synthetic route to prepare nanoparticles (Fig. 6 ). The authors state that the selection of a method for waste treatment depends on several factors, including particle size and chemical composition. Therefore, it is crucial to identify the matrix before choosing a method. For instance, ore tailings, a type of industrial waste, often have high iron content. This iron can be extracted using chemical and thermal treatments to produce an ionic species that serves as a precursor for creating iron-based nanoparticles [79–82].

Machado et al. [83] discovered that a mixture of citrus fruits extract and an iron matrix could produce zero-valent iron nanoparticles due to the solution's reducing nature. Biswas et al. [84] synthesized goethites, magnetites, and hematites with varying morphologies using scrap iron under different conditions of synthesis and sample treatment.

The advantages of co-precipitation over traditional methods are the use of accessible reagents and, from a green chemistry perspective, the absence of by-products and toxic compounds. Besenhard et al. [85] obtained Fe3O4/γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles via co-precipitation using FeCl2 and FeCl3 as precursors. Using a flow reactor system and synchrotron X-ray diffraction monitoring, the researchers found that the sophisticated control characteristic of coprecipitation occurs through the conversion of the precursors into crystalline iron(II) carbonate plates and poorly crystalline ferrihydrite particles, the raw material for the maturation and formation of FeNPs. Using an experimental design, Roch [86] modeled the effects of precursors and pH regulators on the co-precipitation process of superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles. The results showed that the Fe(III)/Fe(II) ratio and the relationship of this ratio to the concentration of hydroxides were associated with the differentiation of different forms of iron oxides and hydroxides (such as goethites, lepidocrocite, and magnetites).

Yazdani and Seddigh [87] investigated the effect of counter-ions on the coprecipitation process of FeNPs. This suggests that the composition of precursors can alter the production of FeNPs. The researchers found that increasing the size of the anion resulted in the formation of smaller magnetite nanoparticles. This finding supports the principles of green chemistry, as controlling the ionic strength allows for atom conservation by eliminating the need for a steric stabilizer.

Carbon, a versatile element, has attracted attention due to its ability to form hybridization states, forming a wide range of allotropes from diamond to graphite. The advent of nanotechnology and the discovery of fullerenes in 1985 led to the exploration of carbon nanostructures, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), and graphene. These nanomaterials have superior electronic, physical, optical, and magnetic properties, and researchers are exploring other carbon nano allotropes in the future. Carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) are a new member of the older generation of carbon nanomaterials due to their small size, ease of preparation, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, excellent chemical stability, and high solubility in both polar and non-polar solvents. These zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures have varying degrees of crystallinity [88]. There exists a broad range of CNPs which can be summarized in Table 2 .
The different methods of synthesis of these NPs will be discussed in the next section.

Carbon nanoparticles can be made using two main methods: bottom-up and top-down (Table 3 ). The bottom-up approach starts with small organic mole­cules, while the top-down me­thod uses larger carbon-based mate­rials [94]. Top-down approaches involve breaking down larger carbon structures into nano- and microparticles using laser ablation, discharge, and electrochemical oxidation to create carbon nanoparticles from basic carbon materials [95]. The bottom-up approach involves the transformation of smaller carbon structures into carbon nanoparticles through hydrothermal and solvothermal reactions, microwave pyrolysis, and ultrasonication [95].

Hydrothermal synthesis is a solution reaction-based method for synthesizing nanomaterials at room-to-high temperatures, utilizing low-pressure or high-pressure conditions to control morphology [96]. Solvent thermal methods use organic solvents heated above boiling points to increase reactor pressure and alter product properties through temperature, pressure, solvent type, and other variables [97]. Further, in microwave pyrolysis, the irradiation creates localized hotspots on carbon char, acting as nucleation sites with the assist of feed material minerals, catalyzing the mechanism. Pyrolysis volatiles self-extrude from the active site and undergo re-solidification, possibly due to secondary pyrolysis or lower outside temperature [98]. Despite all, ultrasonication involves the propagation of high-amplitude ultrasonic pressure waves. The energy in ionic liquids causes the formation, growth, and collapse of microcavities, increasing pressure and temperature, and developing shear forces and sonochemical reactions [99].

In general, the natural carbon sources used in the green synthesis of carbon nanoparticles could be categorized into three main groups: microorganisms, plant-based, and animal-derived, as shown in Table 4 [119].

An example of a microorganism as a carbon source is the cyanobacteria. They are phototrophic bacteria found in eutrophic waters. They prosper in overly polluted water, resulting in a distinct green color. Cyanobacteria are renewable and inexpensive biomass with ample nutrients, making them appealing as a carbon source, and edible mushrooms can be used as a carbon source because they are naturally available, edible nitrogenous fungi that do not require solvents for synthetic processes [120,121]. In addition, plant-based matrices such as bagasse, rice bran, corn stover, etc., and animal-derived matrices such as honey, egg, milk, etc. could also be used as carbon sources [119].

To evaluate the efficiency of green synthesis, Wang employed cyanobacteria biomass as a carbon source to prepare water-soluble carbon quantum dots by applying a simple hydrothermal method. The CQDs were monodispersed, photobleach-free, and had low cytotoxicity to PC12 cells. They were efficient in cell illumination and could distinguish between cytoplasm and nucleus. The green synthesis, low cost, and low cytotoxicity make these CQDs an important carbon source [120].

In addition, Hakim investigated a one-step water-assisted synthesis method to produce carbon nanotubes from graphite flakes derived from coconut shell waste. The carbon nanotubes were characterized using Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The results demonstrate the potential of this green chemistry-based method and denote the possibility of producing carbon nanotubes on a larger scale [136].

However, Surendran utilized honey for the extraction of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which possess a spherical structure with a diameter measuring 8.29 nm. The CQDs were efficiently infused with nitrogen and sulfur atoms, resulting in a blue fluorescence under UV light exposure. The quantum efficiency of honey-derived CQDs was determined to be 4.192 % at the emission wavelength of 431 nm [137].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations and advantages of sustainable nanoparticles synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of carbon, iron, and silver nanoparticles through the application of green chemistry principles holds significant promise for sustainable material production across various domains. This approach emphasizes the utilization of environmentally friendly solvents and reagents, adherence to high atom economy, and minimization of waste generation. While technological progress enables deeper insights into precursor conversion mechanisms, there remains some challenges to be overcome such as: (i) green procedures for commercial scale: industries need to engage with the research community to develop new techniques for scaling up the production of green NPs; (ii) development of toxicology protocols: to ensure the viability of green methods, it is crucial to adhere to toxicology protocols to comply with special regulations; (iii) evaluate the viability of the green methodology: establish the industrial/commercial viability of green methods to avoid or minimize environmental and health impact [142].

Although these limitations restrict the applicability of green methods for the synthesis of Fe, Ag and Carbon NPs, the advantages compared with traditional methods are sufficient to encourage researchers to search for less harmful methods. From a strategic way, these routes also fit into the methodological perspectives of safe-by–design. Furthermore, in addition to the advantages associated with regulator safety mechanisms, the use of green principles ensures process homogeneity and standardization through the use of fewer resources, which are globally available or easily accessible. From an environmental standpoint, the green approach entails not only the use of environmentally safe products but also the implementation of production protocols that limit and minimize the environmental impact of the extraction, production, and marketing of raw materials.

In the last decades, the challenges in this field of research have often been related to the difficulty of standardization (reproducibility) and the availability of chemical resources and methods for the morphological characterization of nanoparticles. However, recent technological advances have overcome these challenges. Currently, it is estimated that the main prospects in the field of synthesis are related to the application of green nanoparticles in functionalized materials for multidisciplinary areas such as energy and health.

The prospects in this field are strongly linked to human needs. In general, these prospects can be summarized in the development of new methods and technologies, including proposals for catalysts and reaction stabilizers for the production of NPs. Improving the integration of nanotechnology with medicine, biology and other areas of health is essential to promote applications that address current problems.

Maycon Lucas de Oliveira: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation.
